meta.status: POPULATED — live web research + training knowledge. DO NOT OVERWRITE — populated by research team.
sources: Belfast City Council — Belfast Castle and Malone House venue information and pricing
NLHF — Clifton House digital lifeline award announcement

Wilton's Music Hall — published charity accounts and press coverage

caseStudies.name: Belfast Castle
keyLessons: Proof of concept — a heritage house in Belfast can sustain a high-occupancy wedding programme
BCC pricing is subsidised and inflexible — independent Fernhill could price at 100–230% premium while remaining below commercial hotel rates
Heritage narrative (Victorian architecture, Cave Hill backdrop) generates self-referral bookings without paid advertising
High weekend occupancy confirms strong unmet demand in wider Belfast heritage wedding market
caseStudies.fernhillImplication: Belfast Castle proves citywide demand for heritage-house weddings. Fernhill replicates the revenue logic with independent charitable governance and a west/northwest Belfast parkland location — a segment Belfast Castle does not serve locally.

caseStudies.name: Malone House
keyLessons: Closest format comparator (Georgian BCC heritage house) — geographic catchment is south Belfast, not Glencairn
Published pricing and package structure are the clearest public-sector benchmark for Fernhill fee-setting
Published room hire rates provide clearest market benchmark for Fernhill pricing decisions
In-house catering model adds revenue per event and allows quality control
Community art gallery on non-wedding days creates footfall and goodwill
caseStudies.fernhillImplication: Malone House is the single most important pricing and format benchmark. Citywide heritage wedding demand is evidenced by BCC occupancy and published rates; Fernhill's case is that west/northwest Belfast lacks an equivalent local heritage-house offer.

caseStudies.name: Clifton House (Poor House), Belfast
caseStudies.location: North Queen Street, north Belfast BT14 7GH
caseStudies.governance: Independent CCNI-registered charitable company — direct model for Fernhill
caseStudies.majorFunders: NLHF, AHF, Historic Environment Division NI
keyLessons: Independent charitable trust model gives governance credibility that NLHF, AHF, and HED NI require
NLHF has funded Clifton House across multiple campaigns — long-term funder relationship is possible
Community history narrative drives fundraising more powerfully than architectural merit alone
Grade A listing unlocks strongest HED NI funding levels
caseStudies.fernhillImplication: Clifton House Trust is the governance and funding model Fernhill should follow directly. Same structure (CCNI charitable company), same primary funder (NLHF), same approach.

caseStudies.name: The Duncairn Centre for Arts & Culture, Belfast
caseStudies.type: Former Presbyterian church — converted to arts and community venue
caseStudies.majorFunders: NLHF, SEUPB PEACE IV, Belfast City Council, Arts Council NI
keyLessons: Cross-community positioning is the most powerful NI funding multiplier — Duncairn's interface area positioning was essential for SEUPB PEACE IV
NLHF + SEUPB combination is proven in Belfast — Fernhill can replicate this stacking
Café as community anchor — low F&B margins but creates daily footfall and community habit
Multi-use programming (arts + community hire + café) covers the full week, not just event days
caseStudies.fernhillImplication: Model the SEUPB application on Duncairn's cross-community approach. Build cross-community commitment into Fernhill's governance and programming from day one — it is both ethically right and a significant funding multiplier.

caseStudies.name: Crumlin Road Gaol, Belfast
caseStudies.type: Victorian gaol — designed by Sir Charles Lanyon (1846)
keyLessons: 150,000 visitors/year proves Belfast's appetite for heritage experiences is substantial
Private commercial operation = maximum commercial freedom but permanently locked out of future NLHF, AHF, HEF NI capital grants
Phased restoration and early opening created commercial momentum before full restoration complete
Creative programming within heritage constraints (Halloween events, crime history tours) shows commercial imagination applied to authentic heritage
caseStudies.fernhillImplication: Key contrast: Fernhill's charitable model sacrifices some commercial freedom for significantly greater ongoing public investment potential. The charitable model is the right long-term choice.

caseStudies.name: Wilton's Music Hall, London
caseStudies.type: Victorian music hall (1858) — oldest surviving music hall in the world
caseStudies.owner: Wilton's Music Hall Trust (independent charitable trust)
caseStudies.majorFunders: NLHF, Arts Council England, Historic England, private donations
caseStudies.restorationApproach: Deliberate retention of 'ruin aesthetic' — exposed brick, peeling plaster — as positive brand asset
keyLessons: Phased restoration + early opening is a strategy not a compromise — Wilton's opened for events before restoration was complete
'Beautiful ruin' aesthetic — genuine Victorian fabric — became the most powerful marketing asset; couples and audiences choose it because it is authentic
Early community engagement while partially restored built the donor base before the major capital appeal
Independent charitable trust governance was non-negotiable for NLHF Heritage Places funding
caseStudies.fernhillImplication: Don't wait for full restoration before opening. Open the tea room in the first habitable rooms. Hold community events in partially restored spaces. Fernhill's genuine Victorian fabric — original cornicing, period joinery, worn stone — is a commercial asset to be celebrated, not a deficiency to be hidden.

summaryInsights: Independent charitable trust governance is the dominant model among all successful cases — required for NLHF and AHF
NLHF is the anchor funder in every major UK heritage restoration — no exceptions in the case study set
Weddings are the primary revenue driver for heritage house venues — Belfast Castle and Malone House confirm this in Belfast specifically
Multi-revenue streams (weddings + events + community + F&B) are essential for financial resilience
Cross-community narrative is a NI funding multiplier — Duncairn demonstrates this most clearly
Phased opening and early community engagement produces stronger long-term outcomes than waiting for completion — Wilton's and Duncairn both demonstrate this
